prompt: Turn my 'Pantry inventory reset' project into a repeating project that comes back on the 1st of every month.
systemPrompt:
You are an assistant helping a user manage their tasks in the Things app (Things 3, by Cultured Code).

You have one tool: a bash shell. The `things` command-line program is installed and on PATH; use it to inspect and change the user's tasks. You have no prior knowledge of how `things` works â€” discover its commands, flags, and output by running `things --help` and `things <command> --help`.

Standard POSIX utilities (cat, grep, jq, sed, ls, ...) are available in the shell for parsing output.

Final-answer protocol: when the task asks a question, finish your reply with exactly one fenced code block tagged `json` containing the answer as a JSON object, as the last thing in your message with nothing after it. When the task only asks you to change something, no json block is required.

A reference skill for the `things` CLI is mounted in the filesystem. Before working by trial and error, read /skill/SKILL.md â€” it documents the data model, the available commands and their flags, output shapes, and safety/recovery notes. It links to files under /skill/references/ (reads, writes, gui, data-model, recurrence, safety-and-recovery); read the ones relevant to your task. The skill describes the same CLI you have; prefer it over rediscovering the interface from --help.

content.text: Turn my 'Pantry inventory reset' project into a repeating project that comes back on the 1st of every month.

arguments.command:
cat /skill/SKILL.md && printf '
---
' && ls /skill/references && printf '
---
' && sed -n '1,220p' /skill/references/data-model.md && printf '
---
' && sed -n '1,220p' /skill/references/recurrence.md && printf '
---
' && things --help && printf '
---
' && things project --help

content.text:
---
name: things-cli
description: Read and manage a user's tasks in the Things 3 app (macOS) through the `things` CLI â€” list views like Today/Inbox/Upcoming, search, and create, edit, schedule, complete, move, or organize to-dos, projects, areas, headings, and tags. Use whenever the user asks about their tasks, to-dos, projects, or anything in Things.
---

# Things CLI

`things` is a command-line interface to the user's Things 3 task database. Reads are instant SQL queries; writes go through the app itself and are verified after they land. Use `things` when it is on your PATH; otherwise substitute `npx -y things-api@latest` in every command (identical subcommands and flags).

`things --help` is the one-screen index; `things <group> --help` lists a group's verbs and flags (always current for the binary you invoke); `things help <topic>` opens a contract guide â€” topics: `agent`, `filters`, `ids`, `output`, `repeating`, `writes`.

## Data model (read this first)

- **To-do** â€” the basic item: title, notes, an optional **checklist** (sub-steps), tags, schedule, deadline, reminder. Has at most ONE container â€” loose in an **area**, directly in a **project**, or under a **heading** inside a project â€” or **none at all** (standalone to-dos are normal, like standalone projects).
- **Inbox** â€” not a container but the *untriaged state*: an inbox to-do has no container and no schedule. Filing it into a container or scheduling it moves it out of the Inbox; moving something back TO the Inbox un-files AND un-schedules it.
- **Project** â€” a goal-sized container of to-dos, optionally divided by **headings** (section labels; a heading belongs to one project and cannot hold projects). Projects live in an area or stand alone. Projects can also have their own notes, tags, schedule, and deadline.
- **Area** â€” a top-level bucket (e.g. a sphere of responsibility) holding projects and loose to-dos. Areas have tags but no dates.
- **Tags** â€” form a hierarchy, and are **inherited downward**: a to-do effectively carries its own tags plus those of its project and area. Headings carry no tags, but inheritance flows through them from project to to-do.
- **Views** are queries over this model, matching the app's sidebar: `inbox` (unsorted), `today` (scheduled for today, incl. This Evening), `upcoming` (future-dated), `anytime` (all active), `someday` (kept without a date), `logbook` (completed/canceled), `trash`.
- **Scheduling vocabulary**: an item's *when* is `today | evening | anytime | someday | YYYY-MM-DD` â€” where the item sits/starts. A **deadline** is a separate due date. A **reminder** is a separate time-of-day alert parameter â€” never write `date@time` into *when*.

## Referring to items

Commands take a `<ref>`: a UUID, a unique UUID prefix, or a (unique) title. Ambiguous refs fail with the candidates listed â€” pick one and retry. Discover UUIDs via any read command; add `--json` for stable machine output (UUIDs are in `.uuid`).

## Stable contracts

These hold regardless of the binary version; see [references/contracts.md](references/contracts.md) for the full text.

- **JSON envelope**: every `--json` response is `{ ok, data, meta }`. Read results from `.data` (usually `.data[]`), never `.items`; UUIDs are `.uuid`, not `.id`. Check `meta.truncation` before concluding "no match" or "that's everything". List/search rows are summaries whose `tags` may be incomplete â€” use `things show <ref> --json` for effective tags, checklist, and placement.
- **Exit codes**: `0` landed and verified Â· `2` usage error Â· `3` verify-failed. Nonzero means the change did NOT stick; the message names the fix.
- **Previews, undo & gates**: `--dry-run` shows any write's exact plan without executing; `things undo` reverses recent changes made through this tool; repeating and other disruptive operations require `--allow-disruptive`, INCLUDING their dry runs.
- **Preconditions**: referenced containers and tags must already exist â€” create nested structures outside-in and reuse each returned UUID.
- **Recurrence**: converting to a *fixed* repeater REPLACES the item, returning a `repeating` block â€” use `instanceUuid` to reach the visible occurrence and `templateUuid` for `reschedule-repeat`. Full vocabulary: `things help repeating`.
- **View reasoning from JSON**: `start:"inbox"`â†’Inbox, `start:"someday"`â†’Someday, `start:"active"` with no `startDate`â†’Anytime; a dated open item is Today when dated for `meta.clock.today`, else Upcoming. `todaySection` only marks placement within Today and is NOT evidence an undated item is in Today. Completed/canceled â†’ Logbook, trashed â†’ Trash, regardless of a stale `logged` field.
- If the user requests a JSON reply schema, return exactly that object after the read or verified write.

## Reading

Views and lookups â€” pass `--json` whenever you will act on the output: `things today | inbox | upcoming | anytime | someday | logbook | trash`, `things show <ref>` (full detail incl. notes + checklist + effective tags), `things projects [ref]`, `things areas [ref]`, `things tags`, `things search <words>`, `things changes --since <moment>`. Filters (`--tag`, `--untagged`, `--overdue`, `--limit N`, â€¦) compose with AND â€” see `things help filters` and [references/model.md](references/model.md).

## Writing

Namespaced verb families â€” run `things <group> --help` for the verbs and `things <group> <verb> --help` for exact flags: `things todo â€¦` (add/update/complete/cancel/reopen/move/delete/restore/tags/checklist/make-repeating), `things project â€¦`, `things area â€¦`, `things heading â€¦`, `things tag â€¦`, plus `things batch` (JSONL of many changes), `things undo`, and `things reorder`.

**Scheduling is an update, not a move**: `things todo update <ref> --when today|evening|anytime|someday|YYYY-MM-DD` schedules or parks an item; `move` changes its CONTAINER only (`--area`/`--project`/`--heading`). Per-verb preconditions and the rest of the write vocabulary: `things help writes`, `things help repeating`, [references/contracts.md](references/contracts.md).

## Going deeper

- [references/model.md](references/model.md) â€” the full data model, view-membership semantics, and filters.
- [references/contracts.md](references/contracts.md) â€” the JSON envelope, exit codes, safety/undo/ambiguity recovery, and the recurrence contract.
- [references/gui.md](references/gui.md) â€” how the user sees Things in the app (where results appear, what list rows show).

---
contracts.md
gui.md
model.md

---
[stderr]
sed: /skill/references/data-model.md: No such file or directory
[exit 1]

arguments.command:
sed -n '1,240p' /skill/references/model.md && printf '
---
' && sed -n '1,240p' /skill/references/repeating.md && printf '
---
' && things help repeating && printf '
---
' && things project --help

content.text:
# The Things data model (deep reference)

Entities, relationships, and how the sidebar views are computed over them, as exposed by the `things` CLI. The SKILL.md "Data model" section is the summary; this is the full version.

## Entities

| Entity | Container | Can contain | Dates | Tags |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| To-do | area, project, heading, or NONE (standalone) | checklist items | when, deadline, reminder | own + inherited |
| Checklist item | its to-do | â€” | â€” | â€” |
| Heading | its project | to-dos | â€” | none (inheritance passes through) |
| Project | area or standalone | headings, to-dos | when, deadline | own + inherited from area |
| Area | top level | projects, loose to-dos | â€” | own |
| Tag | tag hierarchy (may nest) | child tags | â€” | â€” |

## Rules

- A to-do has at most ONE container, and may have none â€” standalone to-dos (no project, no area) are normal and appear at the top level of Anytime/Someday/Upcoming. Moving changes the container; completing or trashing does not.
- The **Inbox is a state, not a container**: "in the Inbox" means untriaged â€” no container AND no schedule. Filing or scheduling an inbox to-do moves it out (filing promotes it to Anytime); moving a to-do TO the Inbox clears both its container and its schedule.
- A heading is a section label inside one project â€” not a task. It cannot be scheduled, tagged, or completed; deleting/archiving it affects only the label, per the operation's contract.
- Tag inheritance flows downward: area â†’ project â†’ (through heading) â†’ to-do. A to-do's *effective* tags = own tags âˆª project tags âˆª area tags. List output distinguishes own vs inherited tags.
- **Status**: open â†’ completed or canceled (both land in the Logbook) or trashed (Trash; restorable until emptied). Reopen brings a logged item back.
- **when** (`today | evening | anytime | someday | YYYY-MM-DD`) controls which view an item appears in; **deadline** is an independent due date shown alongside the item; **reminder** is a time-of-day alert attached to a dated when.
- "Overdue" = open with a deadline strictly before today (a deadline of today is "due", not overdue).

## Views (queries over the model)

`things inbox|today|upcoming|anytime|someday|logbook|trash` mirror the app's sidebar. Each is a query over the rules above, not a stored list:

- **inbox** â€” untriaged to-dos (no container, no schedule).
- **today** â€” items scheduled for today, with the **This Evening** section beneath.
- **upcoming** â€” future-dated items, forward-ordered by date.
- **anytime** â€” all active items kept without a specific date (standalone or filed).
- **someday** â€” items deliberately kept without a date.
- **logbook** â€” completed/canceled items.
- **trash** â€” trashed items (restorable until the trash is emptied).

`things projects`/`areas`/`tags` list containers; `things projects <ref>` / `things areas <ref>` / `things show <ref>` show one item's full detail â€” notes, checklist, effective tags â€” which the compact list rows do NOT display.

### Reading view membership from JSON

When reasoning about which view an item belongs to from `--json`, key on the fields together, not on a single hint:

- `start: "inbox"` â†’ Inbox; `start: "someday"` â†’ Someday; `start: "active"` with no `startDate` â†’ Anytime.
- A dated open item belongs to **Today** when dated for `meta.clock.today`, otherwise **Upcoming** when future-dated.
- `todaySection` only describes placement WITHIN Today (e.g. `evening`); it is NOT evidence that an undated item is in Today.
- Completed/canceled items are in the Logbook and trashed items in Trash, regardless of a stale-looking `logged` field.

## Filters over views

Read filters compose with AND: `--tag <name>` (repeatable) / `--untagged` / `--exact-tag` for tags (in single-container `project show` / `area show` these match the row's own tags; in flat views they include inherited tags), `--overdue` (open items whose deadline is before today), `--limit N`, and `--since`/`--until` where offered. `things search <words>` matches title/notes over open items â€” widen with `--all`, `--logged`, `--trashed`; narrow with `--type project`. `things changes --since <moment>` is the pull-based substitute for a watch mode. Exact flags per command: `things <group> --help` and `things help filters`.

---
[stderr]
sed: /skill/references/repeating.md: No such file or directory
[exit 1]

arguments.command: things help repeating && printf '\n---\n' && things project --help

content.text:
REPEATING â€” rules for recurring to-dos and projects

Verbs: `things todo|project make-repeating <ref>` (turn an existing item into a
repeater), `things project create-repeating "<title>"`, `â€¦ reschedule-repeat <ref>`
(change a rule in place). Every rule takes `--frequency <daily|weekly|monthly|yearly>`
and a required `--interval <n>` (`--interval 1` = every unit). Repeating operations
require `--allow-disruptive`, including `--dry-run` previews.

Two modes:
  fixed (default)      occurrences land on calendar dates. NOTE: this REPLACES the
                       item â€” the response returns the new uuids (see below).
  --after-completion   next occurrence lands N units after you complete the current
                       one. The item keeps its uuid.

Shaping the rule (compose with the frequency):
  --weekdays mon,thu,fri      weekly: MULTIPLE days in ONE rule â€” never make two
                              repeaters for "every Thursday and Friday"
  --on-day <1-31|last>        monthly/yearly: a date anchor
  --on-weekday <day> --on-ordinal <1-5|last>   nth-weekday anchor; yearly adds
  --yearly-month <1-12>       e.g. last Sunday of December, every year:
                              --frequency yearly --yearly-month 12
                              --on-weekday sunday --on-ordinal last
  --ends-after <n> | --ends-on YYYY-MM-DD      stop bounds
  --reminder HH:mm Â· --deadline Â· --start-days-earlier <n>

make-repeating returns a `repeating` block: `instanceUuid` is the visible current
occurrence (use it to reach the item), `templateUuid` the recurring rule (use it as
the <ref> for `reschedule-repeat`), and `replacedUuid` the original when it was
replaced. Typical chain: `things todo add "<title>" --json`, then
`things todo make-repeating <returned-uuid> --frequency â€¦ --allow-disruptive`.

---
Usage: things project [options] [command]

Project-scoped operations

Options:
  -h, --help                          display help for command

Commands:
  show [options] <ref>                Composite project view mirroring the
                                      native UI: active items and headings.
                                      --show-later adds
                                      scheduled/repeating/someday rows inline
                                      under their headings; --show-logged adds
                                      the full logbook. Target by uuid or unique
                                      name.
  open [options] <ref>                Open the project in the Things app on this
                                      Mac (brings the window forward). Errors if
                                      the reference is not a project.
  reschedule-repeat [options] <ref>   Change an existing repeating project's
                                      rule in place (target by uuid or unique
                                      name; the project keeps its identity). Set
                                      the new rule with the flags below; see
                                      `things help repeating`. `things undo`
                                      restores the previous rule.
  pause-repeat [options] <ref>        Pause a repeating project: it stops
                                      spawning new occurrences but keeps its
                                      rule. Reversible with `things project
                                      resume-repeat`.
  resume-repeat [options] <ref>       Resume a paused repeating project: it
                                      starts spawning occurrences again.
  make-repeating [options] <ref>      Turn a project into a repeating one. This
                                      REPLACES the project with a new repeating
                                      series â€” the original disappears and a
                                      fresh recurring project takes its place
                                      (its area is kept; cannot be undone). An
                                      Anytime project with no area is moved to
                                      Someday first (a cleanup-free intermediate
                                      step, shown in --dry-run). Set the rule
                                      with the flags below; see `things help
                                      repeating`.
  create-repeating [options] <title>  Create a project and turn it into a
                                      repeating series in ONE call. Two
                                      operations: the project is created first
                                      and PERSISTS even if the make-repeating
                                      step refuses; then it is promoted (which
                                      drives the GUI). Give --area to place it,
                                      or omit it to create in Someday. The new
                                      repeating project's uuid is printed on
                                      success.
  add [options] <title>               Create a project; its uuid is printed on
                                      success.
  update [options] <ref>              Update a project's
                                      title/notes/when/deadline/reminder. Target
                                      by uuid or unique name â€” a duplicated
                                      project name is refused, listing the
                                      candidates to pick from by uuid.
                                      --append-notes/--prepend-notes join with a
                                      newline (exclusive with --notes).
                                      --reminder needs --when
                                      today|evening|YYYY-MM-DD; when
                                      re-scheduling WITHOUT --reminder an
                                      existing reminder is auto-preserved.
  tags [options] <ref>                Set or extend a project's tags (target by
                                      uuid or unique name). --set REPLACES the
                                      full tag set (an empty value clears all
                                      tags); --add merges with the current tags.
                                      Each tag may be a name or a parent/child
                                      path, and must exist unless --create-tags.
  move [options] <ref>                Move a project (target by uuid or unique
                                      name) to another area, or DETACH it from
                                      its current area (--detach). Status and
                                      schedule are untouched. Unknown areas are
                                      rejected.
  cancel [options] <ref>              Cancel a project (target by uuid or unique
                                      name). Canceling also cancels its open
                                      to-dos, so an explicit --children policy
                                      is required; already-completed children
                                      are never altered.
  reopen [options] <ref>              Reopen a completed/canceled project
                                      (target by uuid or unique name). Its
                                      children stay completed/canceled unless
                                      --restore-children also reopens the ones
                                      that were resolved together with the
                                      project â€” children resolved earlier are
                                      never touched. Exit 3 if any child restore
                                      fails.
  restore [options] <ref>             Restore a TRASHED project IN PLACE (target
                                      by uuid or unique name): schedule, area,
                                      and children all keep their state. Only
                                      trashed projects qualify.
  duplicate [options] <ref>           Duplicate a project (target by uuid or
                                      unique name) INCLUDING its children; the
                                      copy's uuid is printed on success. Not
                                      available for repeating projects.
  complete [options] <ref>            Complete a project (target by uuid or
                                      unique name). Completing also completes
                                      its open to-dos, so an explicit --children
                                      policy is required.
  delete [options] <ref>              Move a project (target by uuid or unique
                                      name) to the Trash; its children go with
                                      it (recover with `things project
                                      restore`).
  help [command]                      display help for command
[exit 0]

arguments.command: things projects --json | jq -r '.data[] | select(.title=="Pantry inventory reset") | {uuid,title,area,when,start,projectType}'

things project make-repeating proj-0070 --frequency monthly --interval 1 --on-day 1 --allow-disruptive --dry-run && printf '\n---\n' && things project make-repeating proj-0070 --frequency monthly --interval 1 --on-day 1 --allow-disruptive --json

content.text:
DRY RUN project.make-repeating
  vector: ui (tier 3)
  invocation: reveal area area-0069 â†’ select the project row (AXSelectedRows) â†’ Items â–¸ Repeatâ€¦ â†’ frequency=monthly, interval=1 â†’ OK
  hazards checked: H-UNKNOWN-DESTINATION, H-PROJECT-REPEAT, H-UI-DRIVE
  expected delta: {"mode":"create","probe":{"title":"Pantry inventory reset","type":"project","sinceEpoch":0},"assert":[{"field":"repeating.isTemplate","equals":true}]}

---
{"apiVersion":1,"ok":false,"kind":"error","error":{"code":"blocked:H-UI-DRIVE","message":"this operation drives the local Things app through the Accessibility API â€” the project row-selection, menu press, and Repeat dialog all run through the Accessibility tree","remediation":"pass dangerouslyDriveGui (--dangerously-drive-gui) to proceed; the vector also requires `things config set ui-enabled true` and Accessibility granted to this process (see docs/setup.md)"},"meta":{"dbVersion":26,"fingerprint":"ok","elapsedMs":4,"clock":{"timezone":"America/Chicago","today":"2026-07-20"}}}
[exit 4]

arguments.command: things config set ui-enabled true && things project make-repeating proj-0070 --frequency monthly --interval 1 --on-day 1 --allow-disruptive --dangerously-drive-gui --json
